data.title: Allspring Large Cap Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The fourth quarter of 2024 marked a strong finish to an impressive year for U.S. equities. The broader economy demonstrated resilience, supported by steady inflation, consumer spending, and earnings growth that largely exceeded expectations. Artificial intelligence emerged as a pivotal driver for growth with companies investing heavily to capitalize on its potential. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) advanced 2.41% for the quarter and 25.02% for the year. Growth stocks maintained their leadership position for the quarter and the year, as noted by the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 33.36% annual return. While mid- and small-cap stocks experienced periods of favorable leadership during the year, they ultimately lagged their large-cap counterparts.

Driving the quarterly returns was an initial bullish reaction to the U.S. presidential election. Investors responded optimistically to the potential for business-friendly policies and faster economic growth. Following the election, there was an immediate rotation into cyclicals, small-cap stocks, and speculative investments such as cryptocurrencies and lower-quality equities.

The post-election fervor wore off later in the quarter as investors realized that key aspects of the Trump policy agenda could create inflationary pressures. When gross domestic product and inflation came in higher than expected, the U.S. Federal Reserve signaled fewer rate cuts than previously anticipated. A “higher-for-longer” sentiment drove the term premium for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield to rise 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) during the quarter. As 2024 came to a close, the bullish enthusiasm faltered over the prospects of rising inflation, higher interest rates, and falling stock prices.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Holdings within IT and industrials detracted from returns. Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), a leading provider of semiconductor and software products, holds more than one-third of the market share within application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs). During the quarter, we initiated a position, yet the relative underweight to the benchmark detracted from returns. We will continue to opportunistically increase the position within Broadcom. GE Aerospace (GE) within industrials is one of the largest providers of engines and aftermarket parts. The company fell short of investors' high expectations during the quarter as supply chain constraints resulted in softer-than-expected guidance. We believe that these delays are temporary and that demand for GE should remain robust for years to come. Select IT and consumer discretionary holdings contributed positively to performance. ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), is on a short list of software companies with revenue derived from existing AI and machine learning products. This resulted in new client and renewal growth exceeding expectations during the quarter. Within discretionary, Booking Holdings, Inc. (BKNG), is a leading global provider of online travel services operating under multiple brands. With convenient mobile apps and unique locations, Booking Holdings has continued to exceed expectations, while other hotel brands have reported mixed results. The company is leveraging AI to reduce expenses and increase operational efficiency while also benefiting from the consumers' preference for experiences.

Portfolio Positioning: Trailing 12-Month Attribution Analysis

The fund underperformed the benchmark over the trailing 12-month period, in part due to holdings within IT and industrials. Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), produces semiconductors, data center components, and computing technology. While AMD has continued to grow revenue and margins, the company has failed to capture market share. As a result, the stock underperformed both its top competitor and the benchmark. We exited the position in favor of a higher-conviction holding. Within industrials, Old Dominion Freight Line, Inc. (ODFL), is one of the largest less-than-truckload shipping companies. Over the past several quarters, the industry experienced a contraction in freight tonnage as retailers focused on destocking inventories. We continue to believe in Old Dominion’s ability to strategically increase market share and produce steady growth for investors. Security selection within communication services along with select IT positions contributed positively to returns. Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) has led the buildout of AI with its graphics processing units and data center processors. The company’s next-generation chip, the Blackwell B2000 GPU, is anticipated to further the company’s exceptional earnings growth. Within communication services, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), has aggressively invested in its social media platform to improve advertising efficacy and operational efficiency. Leveraging AI-driven technology, we believe Meta has cultivated a wide moat within digital advertising and are optimistic on the long-term growth prospects for Meta.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The rising interest rate backdrop created a pause in the broadening of market leadership. While a few speculative corners of markets, such as cryptocurrencies and meme stocks, showed momentum, U.S. equities took a generally defensive turn during the quarter. Mega-cap tech stocks resumed market leadership while small caps and more cyclical and rate-sensitive sectors lagged. Our response to this evolving environment was to remain disciplined.

While policy shifts may trigger short-term rotations, we believe that structural growth scarcity should continue to command premium valuations for high-quality growth companies. We avoid chasing cyclical rallies under the banner of "new regimes" and instead keep our focus on businesses with sustainable earnings growth and strong competitive positions. We believe that security selection—not macroeconomic timing—should drive performance in 2025.

We remain excited about the technological innovation inherent in our portfolio companies. We own businesses with durable competitive advantages at the forefront of driving productivity gains. AI spending should remain a key growth driver, potentially ushering in a super cycle of investment that transcends the IT sector. We expect the next phase of AI should shift from building capacity to practical applications in 2025. Our investment process is designed to capture the strong growth potential of these trends.

data.title: Is It Time To Invest In Europe?
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Going abroad

There has been a lot of pessimism about Europe, given stalling growth, rising costs, tariff threats and the war in Ukraine. Former economic engine Germany has stagnated for two consecutive years and things don't look much better in France, Italy, or elsewhere. Interestingly, European stock markets have performed well as of late, even beating the U.S. since the beginning of the year.

Snapshot: Increased military spending pushed the region's defense stocks to all-time highs on Monday, while the STOXX Europe 600 Index (STOXX) has already climbed 8.6% in 2025, more than double the 4% return of the S&P 500 (SP500). Things look even better for the Euro Stoxx 50 Index (SX5E), which is up more than 14% YTD, and even for France and Germany, where the CAC 40 (CAC:IND) and DAX (DAX:IND) are ahead by 10.4% and 12.4% YTD. This bullishness has surprised many, especially when many nations in Europe are mired in political volatility.

What's going on? European central banks are expected to continue easing as the Federal Reserve stays on pause, while Ukraine ceasefire talks are underway, as well as a push toward stimulus in prior fiscally disciplined countries. European markets are also way smaller than the U.S., so even a little bit of diversification money being transferred there will show an outsized impact. At the recent World Economic Forum, BlackRock's (BLK) Larry Fink called investment in Europe a counter-trade, saying, "Always go against Davos and you'll make a lot of money."

Elsewhere: Germans are going to the polls this weekend, with the CDU's Friedrich Merz poised to be the next chancellor, though he may face a difficult time forming a governing majority due to the rise of Alternative for Germany (AfD). On the ballot are stances over migration, as well as economic malaise prompted by the loss of industry and cheap Russian energy. For investors, there also might be more of a spotlight on reforms to the Schuldenbremse, or debt brake, to finance structural investments for the economy. In terms of foreign markets, Seeking Alpha subscribers actually see more opportunity in India compared to other regions for investment, according to the latest SA Sentiment Survey, though Europe came in second place. Will the gains last?

What else is happening...

007 takeover: Amazon (AMZN) to make future James Bond films.

Citigroup (C) drops diversity goals, 'DEI' term after Trump order.

Beverage jolt: Celsius (CELH) skyrockets on Alani Nu deal.

Rivian (RIVN) posts first gross profit, but expects fewer EV deliveries.

Nissan turns to Tesla (TSLA) after Honda fallout; rating cut to junk.

GameStop's (GME) Ryan Cohen ups Alibaba (BABA) stake to $1B.

Microsoft-backed (MSFT) OpenAI roll out its AI agent Operator.

Walmart's (WMT) executives downplay deflated guidance.

Cruise operators face rough waters under Trump administration.

Disney's (DIS) ESPN, Major League Baseball decide to part ways.

data.title: Tel-Aviv Stock Exchange: An Enticing Moat-Rich Stock Market Operator To Own For Life
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Oliver Mitchell as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Image source: Seeking Alpha suggestion

Before we get started

Currency is in Shekels unless otherwise specified

Links are to the relevant section of their filing, for the sake of ease and due diligence

If you are reading at least a quarter after this article's publication date, make sure to check the recent figures.

Summary

The Tel-Aviv Stock Exchange (OTCPK:TVAVF) (TASE.TA), a 4.4bn mkt cap enterprise, presents an opportunity as an undervalued business with a considerable moat, both on a quantitative and qualitative basis, with very few risks attached. They are a business you could own forever – value creation will continue indefinitely as measured by FCF growth over time (the only driver of stock prices in the long run). The opportunity exists due to the market, once again, overlooking what’s right in front of them. This will all be in more detail in what’s coming, but just so you get the idea.

Note: Links are to the relevant section of their filing, for the sake of ease and DD.

The Business

Business Description

The Tel Aviv Stock exchange (henceforth TASE) is an Israel-based/listed business who manage the only stock exchange in Israel. As a stock market operator, their primary concern day to day is approving the issuance of activities (listings) and providing the relevant data to firms already listed.

Their subsidiaries, as per Their Last Report, are as follows:

TASE Clearing House: the clearing and settlement of their securities (excluding derivatives), meaning they make sure all relevant amounts are paid to the right parties, and that the orders are filled at the appropriate times. Derivatives are far too complex to be worth the trouble, which shows a sound perspective on the side of the management.

MAOF Clearing House: this is where they handle the issuance of options and futures which they excluded from their other subsidiaries’ activities. Their responsibilities are the same as the TASE clearing house, just for different securities, so I’ll avoid repeating myself.

They also act as a Central Securities Depository: they are responsible for holding and managing marketable securities. While it is counterintuitive, it is standard practice as it makes sure ownership is transferred in a proper manner.

The Moat

TASE’s moat is both wide and deep – competitors may try to cross it, but they’re likely to exhaust themselves along the way. This afforded them a lasting competitive advantage:

A legally enforced monopoly within their niche: They are the only stock exchange in Israel. Their markets are unusual in the sense that the listing requirements are extremely low, unlike e.g. the NYSE’s $100mn mkt cap and 3yr earnings min. $10mn requirement, who businesses may choose to IPO on via ADR instead (which has been known to happen incredibly often). This has allowed the TASE market to become comprised of both smaller businesses in need of equity financing along with larger ones. Identical exchanges may come along, but the Israeli regulation is such that it takes years for any competitor to gain their footing. Obviously, TASE aren’t going to lower their drawbridge – they have the reputation of being the only reliable option, so why give that up? In short, it’s highly unlikely (if not impossible) for competitors to invade their niche. It helps that regulators aren’t incentivized to approve another identical market (it makes their job harder, and there’s no real industry progression), and potential competitors aren’t incentivized to try and compete. Competing with TASE is no better of an idea than competing with the NYSE if you had the chance. Reputation, especially of a business with a sound operating history, makes them largely immovable.

Customer Dependency: TASE has foregone the risk typical to most businesses, the risk that customers can be lost at a large scale for no good reason. TASE generates revenue from listing businesses/subscription fees for continuing to be listed. Their subscription model is incredibly simple, but that’s all that’s worth knowing. For TASE to make money, a business must be looking for equity financing for any variety of reasons (which aren’t of relevance here). Managers often forget how to manage the business without the availability of cash, and while they may do very well with it, they’re left with little choice. These leaves TASE continually generating subscription fees with expandable pricing power and minimal revenue downside, providing businesses don’t delist due to fraud or a special situation of some sort (which isn’t even nearly frequent enough to be an issue). For more detail, see the ‘Financials’ section.

Network Effects: As with any market operator, TASE works with broker-dealers. TASE’s reputation, and monopoly of their niche, means brokers have to operate with TASE to do what they’re paid for. They may go to other markets e.g. NYSE, but they would be dealing in different securities there, so it depends on what they want to sell. Due to TASE securities typically being less liquid, manually processed, and pricing set bid-ask spreads, Broker-Dealers typically receive higher commissions, which incentivizes them to deal in TASE securities. History has made it clear they care more about commission than customers, so you get a lot of ignorance there – hence the unrecognized value.

Scalability: due to the subscription model, they already have the infrastructure necessary to list/provide data services to businesses - they don’t have to set up another office, build a factory, or any other usual CAPEX. The marginal effort required is largely a matter of accumulating the right data and documents. This is significantly less than the marginal reward, which is mostly continual payments until the business ends up bust or acquired. This, essentially, is operating leverage. We’re now offered an apt explanation for their past, and likely continual, margin expansion.

The inverse nature of the bet: By investing in TASE, you’re essentially betting on private businesses being poorly managed, and so being reliant on equity financing. This means that, unlike 99.9% of other businesses (arbitrarily speaking), TASE are likely to benefit more in a depressed economy, so when businesses are more likely to need cash (there will be a slight lag). The retort I’d expect is ‘but shouldn’t that even out, as they lose out when things look/are more prosperous?’, to which I would firmly disagree. Their subscription fees will continue to hedge their revenue change. In short, they win when things look bleak, and barely lose out when things look brighter, which provides them (and so, by extension, investors) with an enticingly asymmetric corporate risk-return profile.

Financials

Note: any averages, unless otherwise stated, go back to 2019 – this is when their publicly available data goes back to.

Income Statement

TASE generates revenues mostly based on a subscription service (about 40% of revenues, based on the strictest definition), which affords TASE largely recurring revenues. Their subscription works as a typical model – investors/businesses require all the services so they have the information necessary to abide to, and make best use of, the listings. Their subsidiaries are more likely to be subject to reinvestment than not, for the sake of improvement, but the only upkeep required is updating their large information database and making sure their websites don’t crash (which has proved more than manageable). The downside of any subscription model is the trade-off between growth and stability. TASE's income statement is not likely to see any sudden increase in growth rate, but I see no issues with a slow and steady compounder. As TASE is relatively undervalued (see ‘Comps Valuation’), the growth is a bonus to our returns. I suppose, by this point, you’re probably wondering what the numbers are, so without further ado:

TASE’s revenue, as established, is positively correlated with how many businesses list/stay listed on TASE markets, albeit somewhat cyclically. New listings often follow a depressive general environment of some sort (2023 saw a 3% decrease in new listing fees, which is indicative of new listings – prices haven’t changed meaningfully. This is a return to a more normalized state). New Listings, indicating the allocation ability of private managers, has increased 10%* on average each. So, while there’s been significant volatility in listings, subscriptions are the vast majority of revenues (I’ll avoid repeating myself).It is odd that, while growth varied, there hasn’t been a down year since 2019, nor in net income (as far back as data is available, but I’d take it with a pinch of salt). The revenue increase is 10.5% CAGR, which isn’t ideal, but given the circumstances, is acceptable.

*including dual securities

TASE's 2024 operating margin was 28%. Their average is 19% (rounded), which has been pushed down due to margin expansion at 28.7% CAGR. They have been allocating capital towards reinvesting into the business. Exchange businesses tend to follow this pattern, but these reinvestments are a longer-term bet on cash generation, so it’s not worrying. Given at least a year, operating margins could return to the normalised levels (base case), or possibly peak if this speculation’s history repeats itself.

Net income followed a similar pattern - TASE has demonstrated the ability to generate satisfactory profit on their operation. Their average net margin is 15% (again pushed down), in which the trend has been satisfactory, but by no means smooth. The average net income growth is 42%, which is that operating leverage again. There have been no down years, mainly because of that too. Any business that experiences completely linear growth comes across as Enron-esque. TASE is good, but not too good to be true. If you wait long enough, something will go wrong eventually.

Balance Sheet

Liabilities

TASE's balance sheet is incredibly simple – there are very few items to consider (as is typical of exchanges).

Management has done excellently in ensuring TASE maintain low debt levels (something they don’t share with most of their peers of similar activity, who tend to attempt expansions into more niches). There has only been debt the last two years, but cash/debt has been 5.3 and 8.5 respectively. Usually, there’s more to say on debt, but it’s hard when it’s negligible. It is not entirely clear to me what point there was in taking on this debt – they could have done just fine without it.

The majority of their liabilities are loan or deferred revenue related. This is typical of exchanges, due to the subscription model.

There are no off-balance sheet arrangements or preferred shares.

Assets

Being a software-based business, their tangible asset requirements are incredibly low.

TASE, as you may have guessed, has a stable balance sheet. They’re somewhat capital intensive, which puts some pressure on their cash pile, but they have enough to cover total liabilities. Their cash pile has grown annually at 10% CAGR, which I expect to continue, especially as markets continue to become more speculative. See the Cash Flow Statement section for what they could do with this cash.

It shouldn’t come as a surprise that their non-current assets are mostly intangible, the most important being property and equipment: lease right-of-use assets, deferred tax, and IP. The proportions of these have stayed constant enough over the years to be contributed to the nature of their business model. These non-current assets are a necessity for generating a revenue, which isn’t going to change, most likely ever.

Generating and Allocating cash

TASE have paid an 8% dividend in 2024, with an average dividend coverage of 1.55. The dividend yield has varied over time in tandem with operating results, but nothing out of tune e.g. the 2024Q1-3 dividend yield was 1% (a 75% decrease from the year prior), which closely matches the decrease in the dividend payment. They pay a dividend of a size they think is appropriate, when/if required as the best use of cash. If the benefits of paying dividends are to be accrued, paying them consistently is their first port of call.

I would prefer, mostly for the tax-free nature, for repurchases to be prioritised. While repurchases have been executed when the management believes the stock is below their guess at fair value (which isn’t always, necessarily, accurate), that hasn’t been consistent YoY. There has been net repurchases, with shares outstanding decreasing 10% total since the 2019 IPO.

There is nothing more which reveals the value present here.

Intrinsic Value

TASE, even at an initial glance, is obviously undervalued. In terms of how much, a crude Intrinsic Value calculation yields insight.

My assumptions were as follows:

So, we have a likely 2-4 bagger staring us in the face. There’s no specified timeline, which is the main issue with value acting as its own catalyst. The market could be ignorant for a week, but it could also be ignorant for a lifetime, so let’s not waste any more time thinking about it. I’m not in favour of this condition, hence the applied margin of safety, but it’s worth taking with a pinch of salt. You can only think about so much and remain effective, and this doesn’t fit the mental budget in the slightest.

Comp Valuation

TASE, based on its P/FCF, is undervalued relative to its peers. Considering cash flows are all that drives stock prices in the long run (provided the absence of long periods of mania, which is always possible), this is appealing. So, while there’s some variation, it’s much more to do with that country’s economic situation than anything else.

The industry Price/Free Cash Flow multiples for comparable firms by activity are as follows:

Note: Oliver Mitchell (Author), data from 19/2/2025, source: Yahoo!

TASE's P/FCF is 25. If they reach the industry average (which they should eventually, if market history upholds), we’re presented with a 64% upside*. I prefer using an industry average - it eliminates having to think about if each business considered is over/under valued, which can be done at a glance, but to no accurate extent. The less considered the better - there are less chances for mistakes that way.

I’d take into account, when looking at things yourself, the fact that some of these firms trading well above the average are, of course, overvalued (hence our using the average). Whether you prefer using intrinsic value or relative multiples, the opportunity is clear enough anyway. If you are trying to come to a decision, my advice is this: use a relative multiple. Intrinsic value runs on more assumptions, and making more decisions complicates things. Superior investment returns are earned by keeping things largely simple – you can assess something much better when you only have to think about a few things. It does not get any riskier either way, at least on a price basis, but we must minimize the real source of risk: poor consideration of our investments.

*Note: I cannot give a timeline on when this value will be realized – people are too unpredictable, but the opportunity is not diminished.

Management

Judging by the moat (qualitative and quantitative) TASE has developed, it’s apparent their management is broadly effective (the management composition hasn’t really changed as far back as we look). They’re critical thinkers and honest people – they address their mistakes first, and more importantly, how they came about, and what’s being done about them. While looking for management characteristics is always a very personal affair, there are traits which anyone should agree with.

The management is granted stock options, which is a crucial tell. The manager/shareholder interests must be aligned. If they’re not, it is highly likely shareholders will lose out before any value is realized. There are many ways a business can go, so we must make sure objectives are aligned. If they weren’t, I’d avoid the stock. If I get any inkling that this is starting to change, I will issue an update immediately.

There’s not been much change in board composition recently, which is another positive tell. In order for management to run a business well, they must immerse themselves in it for a long period of time. They have to understand it like they would a person with all their complications. The management here has clearly done that, hence the exceptional state of TASE.

Risks

IPOs slowing: If IPOs were too slow, TASE would forego listing fee revenue, which is consistently of at least mild importance. I say ‘mild’ because, per 2024Q3 data, listing fees are averaging 21% of revenues, which makes a material difference to the cash flow generation of the business. This is not a large risk– businesses, as a class, will always be poorly managed, and otherwise they wouldn’t need to list in larger flocks each economic cycle. If this ever changes, the investment environment is completely changed, and all investing ceases to exist. History has made it clear this risk will stay mild (see new listing figures, income statement section) – the world is far too competitive and fast-paced. Stock exchanges are unusual businesses in the sense that their environment doesn’t really change at all to any meaningful extent, so they largely forego this risk.

There are c.700 listed businesses on the TASE, which is relatively low compared to comps. This, to a fair extent, concentrates their bets. You could say that there’s enough business listed to be diversified, but that disregards the fact that businesses often move as a class. This means that, there is a slight risk, businesses have a change of heart. However, as was made clear in the moat section, there is little reason this risk would materialize. Even if it did, the value change would likely be negligible.

Regulation: The problem with regulation is that, while timely announcements and news coverage dress it up as an orderly affair, it’s really one big mess. Looking at the history of exchanges and businesses within them, you’d quickly realize that there isn’t always the most justifiable reason for these sudden changes. My favorite example of this is how it, solely for smaller businesses, (due to low op costs) can be cheaper to violate regulations than pay most some of the fees (which do vary, and can bankrupt them if they continually do this). If there are slow changes, TASE’s management has enough time to figure out their first port of call to protect themselves, but the same could not be said for the sudden ones. This may lead to TASE having to restrict some activities/be more in depth in others, which would change the long-term composition of their revenues. If this occurs, the hedge they get from subscriptions being most of their revenue may dwindle, and their moat eroded. Due to its unpredictable nature, I can’t provide specifics, but the idea is that their top/bottom line will be challenged. This would harm their CF generating ability, which may slow down TASE being recognized for its merits, and erode our value along the way.

Note: I excluded risks that aren’t idiosyncratic to the business - they don’t bear on TASE’s merits. There’s little point in devoting them any thought beyond deciding to ignore them – they are too unpredictable and generalized.

Approach for Purchase

My approach is to buy as long as it's relatively cheap on a P/FCF basis compared to its peers, and there’s no dissuading material changes. If it reached the industry average, I’d revaluate the opportunity at hand, but that wouldn’t necessarily dissuade me of the business’s merits (I’d be more likely to focus on intrinsic value after that). If it drops, I’d continue to buy the stock up until I feel my other positions are too diluted, which wouldn’t take long (if I have an idea I like, the only sensical option is to act on it in proportion to how appealing it is). I have no other price targets or deadlines in mind – I prefer to keep things simple, and I’m happy to hold this for as long as I’m investing in general (providing no adverse material changes). It’s very obviously undervalued, and there’s enough margin to forego the need for a specific target price, no matter what investment case you use.

Other Points of interest

Avg 3-month Daily Volume: 18724619 ($5.3mn) (volume is listed in Ila, so currency conversions were done on an approximate basis).

Idea Suitability: PA or small fund managers

Mutual Funds (c.51) will have it as part of an index of some sort, in which something like Old Westbury Small & Mid-Cap Strategies Fund stands out as a good general representation. Their institutional ownership stands at 35% (per Yahoo!)

Analyst coverage of any depth is limited, which is mainly due to a $1.24bn mkt cap. It’s just not enticing enough for the ‘analysts’ out there – TASE wouldn’t write them the check (their focus remains on value creation).

Annual Report release is in March (dates vary largely within the month), which may help clear the picture for the rest of the market. They also offer weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual ‘reviews’, which I recommend anyone read to gain an understanding of the merits of their business. It will not take you long.

I don’t hold a position in TASE, nor am I affiliated with the company in any way through employment or communications.

Catalyst

Value can act as its own catalyst, and in most cases it does (whether it is direct or indirect is another matter entirely). The market will eventually recognize the mistake they have made (and if it doesn’t, value investing needs a rethink). It should become more obvious to them as free cash flows (AKA value creation) outpace prices, but until then, our opportunity will expand with time.

Market review

The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) gained 2.41% during the fourth quarter of 2024. Led once again by mega-cap, technology-focused companies, U.S. equities moved higher over the removal of the U.S. election uncertainty, solid economic growth, lower rates, healthy corporate earnings, and expectations of a more business-friendly regulatory environment. West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices rose 5%, U.S. large caps outperformed small caps, and growth outperformed value.

Persistently high shelter costs pushed November’s annualized rate of U.S. headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) inflation back up to +2.7%, while annualized core CPI inflation held steady at 3.3%. New U.S. job growth rebounded in November, while the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.2%. U.S. annualized real gross domestic product (GDP) expanded by 3.1% during the third quarter of 2024. The consensus estimates for fourth quarter real GDP growth are now ~3%, reflecting growing confidence in the health of the U.S. economy.

In December, the Federal Open Market Committee reduced the federal funds rate by another 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), down to a target range of between 4.25% and 4.50%. However, in an increasingly hawkish shift, the U.S. central bank projected only two additional 25-bp rate cuts in 2025, lowering the federal funds rate to approximately 3.90% by the end of 2025.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Fund characteristics included: 50 holdings, a target sector weight +/-5% relative to the benchmark, smaller average market cap versus the S&P 500 Index, and identification of attractively valued companies with improving earnings fundamentals relative to the benchmark. The fund returned 5.04% for the quarter, while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned 2.41%. The fund’s outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within the industrials, financials, and health care sectors. Negative stock selection within the consumer discretionary and energy sectors detracted from performance. United Airlines (UAL) is a major U.S. airline carrier that is more focused on international and long-haul travel relative to its large U.S. peers. United Airlines delivered third quarter earnings ahead of consensus estimates, provided encouraging forward guidance, and announced a new share repurchase program. The solid results were driven by easing fuel prices and an improving domestic revenue environment. Although discount airlines are struggling, United has been opportunistic post-pandemic, focusing on international service, premium products, and scale. United offers the best in-flight connectivity in the industry, which is a differentiator for higher-yielding corporate travelers. United Airlines has remained attractively valued and has maintained a solid balance sheet, and management is well positioned to deploy capital for further share buybacks. PulteGroup (PHM), one of the largest homebuilders in the U.S., reported third quarter revenue and earnings ahead of consensus estimates. However, shares sold off over Pulte’s lower-gross-margin guide for the fourth quarter that highlighted concerns that elevated incentives will affect margins amid an affordability-challenged environment. Pulte has remained attractively valued and has maintained a solid balance sheet and peer-leading margins. We are confident Pulte’s management team should continue executing and capitalize on the secular trends favoring home ownership.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

As we enter 2025, it appears the U.S. has achieved a soft economic landing. Continued disinflation, lower interest rates, and a strong labor market have eliminated most near-term U.S. recession risks. In September 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates for the first time since the 2020 onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, concluding the Fed’s fastest tightening cycle in more than four decades. However, the persistence of inflation, strong economic growth, and low unemployment should force the U.S. central bank to take a more measured approach in 2025. Despite the looming threat of a tariff war by the incoming Trump administration, U.S. GDP growth will likely continue outperforming other developed markets, as monetary policy may shift rapidly from a significant headwind into an economic tailwind.

Since mid-July, equity market returns have broadened out from the “Magnificent Seven” (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla), as the “other 493 stocks” have performed relatively well. After the concentrated mega-cap rally of 2023 and throughout most of 2024, equity markets in 2025 are likely to be defined by slowing global economic growth, single-digit earnings growth, and a broadening of returns. We think that volatility is likely amid a looming tariff war and ongoing geopolitical tensions surrounding Ukraine, the Middle East, and Taiwan. Given this backdrop, we expect 2025 to provide opportunities for disciplined stock selection to add value as it did during 2024. We currently anticipate volatile and range-bound markets and are mindful to neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are generally cautious, there are potential positive catalysts from decelerating inflation, lower rates, a resilient U.S. job market, improving corporate earnings, and a broadening of market returns.

data.title: 3 Reasons I Bought More Realty Income Before Earnings And So Should You
data.description:
Nothing pleases me more than pointing out Buffett-style "Fat pitches" to readers and subscribers.

Wait for a fat pitch and then swing for the fences." - Warren Buffett

Let me explain what this famous Buffett quote means to me. It doesn't mean "time the exact bottom of an undervalued blue chip."

I was pounding the table for about two years on AbbVie (ABBV) as it became undervalued and then kept on getting more undervalued.

Note that the growth rates of earnings and dividends at the time were double digit.

That's how I knew that AbbVie wasn't a "value trap" and that the 4%, 5%, and eventually 6% yield (at its bottom in August 2019) was an incredible opportunity.

I didn't try to guess the bottom; I aggressively recommended and bought AbbVie and trusted that, eventually, the market would realize that 7.5X earnings for a company growing at double digits were absurd.

  • 7.5X earnings prices in -1% EPS growth forever, according to Ben Graham.

If the market is pricing in -1% growth and the actual growth rate is over 10%, that's a "fat pitch" on a dividend aristocrat blue chip like this.

And guess what? Eventually, after years of falling prices and "ugly charts" this "falling knife" became a Wall Street darling, delivering 3.5X returns and 25% annual returns.

Buffett-like returns from a blue-chip (dividend aristocrat) bargain hiding in plain sight for five years.

Today, I wanted to share another Buffett-style fat pitch, Realty Income (NYSE:O).

Earnings are coming up on Feb. 24 (Monday before the bell), and given the incredible discount Realty is trading at right now, it's a coiled spring with very low expectations baked in.

I've Been Buying Realty With Both Hands Ahead Of Earnings

Some might ask, "How can you be so bullish on Realty Income if it's 2% of your portfolio?" Remember that I first pointed out Realty Income's incredible opportunity just six weeks ago, in early January (100-share opening buy).

  • Why Realty Income's 6% Yield Makes It A Must-Buy For Dividend Investors

I started out with a modest starter position and then bought both with limit buys and whenever I had money available each week (usually on Fridays).

Why am I so heck bent on accumulating Realty Income at a yield near 6%?

3 Reasons I Bought Realty Income Before Earnings And So Should You

Here's the TLDR (too long, didn't read) summary in one table.

Realty Income is a low volatility, defensive (and trade war resistant) Ultra SWAN quality dividend aristocrat trading at a nearly 30% discount with 47% fundamentally justified upside potential in the next year alone.

That means that "If and only if Realty Income grows as analysts expect and returns to historical fair value, then if it is 47% higher in 12 months' time, this will be 100% justified by fundamentals and not a bubble."

Analysts expect 16% CAGR returns (154% returns over the next five years).

5 Year Consensus Return Potential

3-Year Consensus Return Potential

Five-year returns are better than the S&P has delivered since the Great Recession, and three-year return potential is better than Buffett's historical 21% CAGR returns.

Reason One: The Ultimate High-Yield Monthly Paying Ultra Sleep Well At Night Blue-Chip

Realty Income is one of the oldest REITs in the world, having been founded in 1969. If you count the time it began paying dividends to private investors, its dividend growth streak is 54 years.

It's an unofficial dividend king, with a dividend growth streak only exceeded by Federal Realty Trust (FRT).

  • 57-year streak.

This is a REIT that IPOed in 1994 with less than 700 properties and has now grown its property base to over 15,000, diversifying into new sectors, new continents, and even into private equity.

Realty is one of just eight REITs with an A-credit rating, giving it a strong competitive advantage in the cost of capital.

Metric 2023/2024 Data
Dividend Yield 5.5–6%
AFFO Payout Ratio 76.3%
Credit Rating A3 (Moody’s)/A- (S&P)
10-Yr Total Return 13.5% CAGR
International Exposure ~10% of rent

(Source: Realty Income)

But what really makes Realty stand out is the quality of its management.

CEO Sumit Roy has been CEO since 2018 and has been with the company for 14 years. In addition to his MBA from the Chicago Booth School of Business, he has a master's in Computer Science from the University of Chicago.

His career spans Cap Gemini (a tech consultancy) and investment banking at Merrill Lynch and UBS.

While Mr. Roy's career primarily focuses on public real estate investment trusts, his experience at UBS Investment Bank exposed him to private real estate companies (C-Corps), which provided him with valuable insights into the dynamics of private equity real estate.

At UBS Investment Bank, he likely gained knowledge of:

Private Equity Transactions: This would have included exposure to various private equity transactions in real estate, such as leveraged buyouts, venture capital investments, and mezzanine financing.

Deal Structuring: He likely participated in structuring complex real estate deals, involving negotiations with private equity firms, developers, and lenders.

Due Diligence: His role at UBS would have required him to conduct due diligence on private real estate companies, assessing their financial performance, operational efficiency, and investment potential.

Why is private equity so valuable to Realty today?

Realty Income isn't just a retail REIT; it now invests in global properties. Thanks to Roy's private equity expertise, he put together a $1 billion deal to buy 22% of the Bellagio in Las Vegas.

You can only get this kind of deal-making with a master of inter-industry expertise, which can only be achieved by banking private and public markets.

The company typically acquires between $1–$3 billion of properties per year, but is very opportunistic.

  • 2021 saw more than $6 billion in acquisitions, excluding the VEREIT merger, and 2023 was a record year with $9.5 billion in investments closed at a 7.1% average cash cap rate.

And how Mr. Roy has a new plan to keep Realty growing at its historical 4% to 6% rate, not just for a few years but potentially for decades.

Reason Two: 5% to 6% Long-Term Growth For The Foreseeable Future (Possibly For Decades)

Sumit Roy has created a private equity strategy for Realty Income, that harkens back to its private days before 1994.

This is when it grew the fastest, achieving cap rates that today's REITs can only dream of.

Realty's new private equity plan is to partner with pensions, sovereign wealth funds, endowments, and insurance companies.

Only long-term capital is similar to investors who invest with Brookfield or Berkshire.

Private equity investors, utilizing the longest-term capital from investors like these, can access 4.9% to 5.5% average cap rates, similar to what Realty is enjoying today.

So why bother with private over public? If there isn't a valuation discount and higher investment returns?

Because the private market is $18.8 trillion in size, 9X bigger than the $1.8 trillion public REIT market.

That means Realty Income's growth potential, historically 4% to 6%, can now be extended potentially up to 9X longer.

And that's not even counting overseas expansion and expanding into other industries (as Realty has been doing for decades).

From a pure focus on standalone retail, Realty Income is now expanding into medical properties, industrial properties, gaming, global properties, and even data centers.

Sumit Roy's vision is to take Realty's incredible scale, and financial stability, providing it with a coveted A-credit rating and $5.7 billion in liquidity, into decades of steady 4% to 6% growth.

That means the key to investing in Realty Income is simple.

  • What long-term total return/income growth do you need?

In the long-term yield + growth drives long-term returns, with multiple expansion a more speculative (but in the case of Realty's well-earned) boost.

If Realty is yielding 5% and growing 5% then 10% long-term returns (market average) but with lower volatility and monthly income is what you can expect.

If you can grab Realty at 6% yield, then you're looking at 11% returns long-term.

But remember that Realty Income is 30% to 35% undervalued meaning that over the next 30 years is an impressive 1.4% CAGR.

In other words, for the next 30 years (retirement time frame for many investors) the long-term total return potential for Realty Income is:

  • 5.8% yield + 5.2% growth (11%) + 1.4% valuation boost = 12.4% CAGR.

For context, that's almost as good as the Nasdaq's 40-year 13.5% CAGR return, but with a lot lower volatility, plus generous monthly dividends.

Reason Three: A Wonderful Company At A Wonderful Price

We've already seen how Realty Income trading at 12.9X FFO is a huge discount compared to its 19.5X 10-year average.

That's what gives Realty Income almost 50% upside potential in the next year alone. But what if I told you that reality might actually be even more undervalued?

How undervalued?

Realty Income Cash-Adjusted PE/(G+Y) Analysis

Realty Income's PEGY ratio divides its Enterprise Value/EBITDA (cash-adjusted earnings) by its yield + LT growth consensus.

  • Cash-adjusted earnings per unit of expected future total returns/Income Growth.
10-Year Median Yield 4.40%
10-year CAGR FFO/Share Growth 5.15%

10-Year Median PEGY Ratio 2.13
16-Year Median Yield 4.55%
16-Year FCF/Share Growth 6.59%
16-Year Median EV/EBITDA 19.45
16-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.75
Current Yield 5.75%

Current PEGY Ratio 1.37
PEGY Overvaluation -35.6%
Upside To Fair Value 55.17%
12-Month Forward EPS Growth 5.00%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified Total Return Potential 60.17%

(Source: FactSet)

The PEGY ratio factors in balance sheets, cash, quality, profitability, growth, and yield; in other words, all the important fundamentals.

In the last 17 years, it has accounted for 91% of stock market returns.

What does the PEGY ratio say about Realty Income?

It's not 28% undervalued but 36% undervalued, a Buffett-style "fat pitch" ultra-value buy.

In other words, when an Ultra SWAN quality company is 35% undervalued, it is historically time to buy with both hands.

Realty Income's discount is so substantial that if Realty grows as expected in the next year (5%) and returns to the historical PEGY ratio, then a 60% rally would be 100% justified.

  • Not a forecast.
  • But that's an Amazon (AMZN) like fundamentally justified return potential.

Realty Income is a good deal when it trades at a 5% yield. It's a great deal when it trades at a 5.5% yield. But if you can buy at a 6% yield or more, shut up and buy!

If you compare Realty's yield to that of its industry peers, 38%, it looks expensive. But remember, it's the gold standard of quality, with one of just eight A-credit ratings, and no peer has a private equity fund sourcing future growth opportunities.

In other words, you sometimes see an analyst on CNBC say, "Stock X is attractive compared to its industry average PE of Y."

I don't care about Realty's yield or valuation vs its peers. I care about its valuation compared to its market-determined objective historical fair value.

If you compare Realty's current yield of 4.56% to its historical yield, then today's yield is a 21% historical discount.

The FFO discount (12.9 vs 19.5 average) is 34%.

And the PEGY ratio discount is 36%.

Whether you look at yield, cash flow multiple, or PEGY ratio, Realty is a screaming buy. Its valuation is at least the best it has been in three years.

For anyone saying, "Realty Income will yield 7% in a bear market," keep in mind that since 2012, the last 13 years, its highest yield was 6.49%.

Even in the depths of the pandemic, when many of its customers were closed down by government orders, Realty Income never once hit a 6.5% yield.

Because the fundamental risk for real estate today is far less than it was then - my limits on real estate are aggressive.

$2.7 Million Worth of Limits For Realty Income: How's That For Conviction?

Do I expect Realty Income to fall by 15%, and that's why I have my last limit set there? Nope.

I'm merely setting rules-based limits that will ensure that whoever Realty Income bottoms in future corrections, I will take advantage of this historic opportunity.

I always update my highest conviction core holding limits ahead of earnings.

  • And set -5%, -10%, and -15% limits right before earnings (Friday night in the case of Realty which reports Monday before the bell).

But that doesn't mean Realty might not suffer a short-term decline.

Risk Profile: What Might Cause Realty Income To Fall In The Next Few Months

The No. 1 myth around Realty Income (and REITs in general) is that "rates up REITs down."

Ten-year yields might be critical to bond yields and returns but no stock is a true bond alternative.

And the REIT industry actually soared in the 1970s during a period of high and rising interest rates.

The investment spread between the cost of capital and cap rates on new properties is what drives long-term growth rates.

Whether borrowing costs are 1%, 11%, or 111%, Realty Income's growth rate will remain strong as long as its investment spread is stable.

Just like debt is meaningless and debt/EBITDA is very meaningful, if 10-year yields are high and rising, that's not bad for Realty Income.

But the market is famous for short-term knee-jerk overreactions and "everyone knows REITs are bond alternatives!"

So, how bad could 10-year yields get in 2025?

Risks To Higher 10-Year Yields

Factor Potential Impact on Yields Magnitude of Impact Key Considerations Examples/Data Points
Trade War Upward pressure due to inflation and potential economic slowdown; Downward pressure due to safe-haven demand and stronger dollar Moderate to High Severity of trade restrictions, impact on global growth, Federal Reserve response, and investor sentiment Tariffs could reduce U.S. GDP growth by 0.36% and increase PCE inflation by 0.4%
Inflation Fears Upward pressure due to expectations of higher inflation and potential Fed tightening Moderate to High The trajectory of CPI and PCE indices, supply chain disruptions, and wage pressures 10-year yield surged to its highest since April after ISM services index showed rising inflation
Increase in Term Premium Upward pressure due to increased risk and uncertainty Moderate Economic outlook, inflation expectations, yield curve dynamics, and quantitative tightening Term premium has been trending higher, but remains below historical averages
High Deficits Upward pressure due to increased government borrowing and debt sustainability concerns Low to Moderate Size of budget deficits, debt-to-GDP ratio, investor sentiment, and election outcomes 10-year yield rose significantly on election day amid fiscal uncertainty

(Source: Morningstar, RealEconomy, Lipper Financial, Committee For Responsible Budget)

How high might 10-year yields go?

The consensus among reputable analysts from T. Rowe Price (TROW), ING, and Schwab is 5% to 5.5%.

For context, 5.0% exactly was the cycle high set in October 2023, and if we were to hit 5.5% yield (depending on how quickly we get there), Realty Income (and REITs in general) MIGHT potentially fall further.

A broader market correction (triggered by anything) could trigger a 5% decline for Realty (6% yield), while a sharp spike to 5% to 5.5% yield in 10-year treasury yields might potentially cause a 10% decline in a bear market (realistic worst-case scenario).

For context, today, Realty is 12.9X FFO, and at 6.5% yield (Pandemic intra-day low), it would be 11X FFO.

A global margin call could potentially drive it even lower, but Realty's P/FFO has been trending higher since its IPO.

Historically, when Realty Income hits 15X P/FFO, it's a significant bargain. Since the Great Recession ended, 14X FFO has been "ringing the bell at the bottom of corrections."

Recently, Realty hit even lower valuations and remains trading at "Shut up and buy it!" valuations.

But valuation alone never means the bottom is in, and the price is about to recover.

But the longer such valuations last, the more we can buy at these historically attractive levels.

Bottom Line: Buying Realty Ahead of Earnings Is Buying A Coiled Spring Before The Next Obvious Catalyst

I'm not saying that Realty Income will crush earnings expectations (like Nvidia might) and take off like a rocket.

I am saying that just like AbbVie was a "Buffett-style fat pitch" in August 2019, Realty Income at a P/FFO of 14X or less is consistent with correction bottoms.

  • 5.1% yield = 14X FFO.

That's why, as long as its yield is 5% or higher, I'll be buying Realty Income every chance I get and setting the most aggressive limits I've ever used in my portfolio.

data.title: Allspring International Equity Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

In the fourth quarter, global developed market equities, as measured by the MSCI World Index (Net), returned -0.16%. Developed international markets, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index (Net), returned -8.11%, with performance negatively correlated with the rising U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR, DXY). U.S. large-cap equities advanced, as measured by the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX), returning 2.41%, as the "Magnificent 7” (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla) regained leadership in the quarter. However, the broader U.S. market lost momentum in December, giving up earlier gains amid concerns over government deficits, tariffs, and inflation. Despite this, U.S. markets still outperformed international markets as investors favored growth over more cyclical economies and sectors.

The fourth quarter performance differential between the MSCI EAFE Index (Net) and the S&P 500 Index has contributed to a large valuation gap, with EAFE trading at a 35% discount to the S&P 500, as measured by 2025 price/earnings (P/E) ratios. We feel there is opportunity offered by current levels in international holdings for investors to rebalance allocations where appropriate. In addition to compelling valuation, there are potential catalysts for developed countries outside of the U.S. that we feel are being underappreciated in the current environment. As in the U.S., many companies overseas are expected to benefit from advances being made in artificial intelligence. Additional catalysts include the potential end of the war in Ukraine; ongoing rate cutting cycles by numerous central banks, including the Bank of England and the European Central Bank; a strengthening U.S. dollar potentially lifting earnings of foreign companies with U.S. sales; increased Chinese government stimulus; and easing inflation.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

We expect macroeconomic uncertainty to continue in 2025. While inflation is trending downward in most developed countries and certain central banks have started to reduce interest rates, most inflation measures remain above target levels. Political change is a common theme in many developed countries, contributing to uncertainty and evolution across key policy dimensions, as are escalating geopolitical tensions. Changing interest rates are contributing more to volatility, whether from valuation implications of near-term rate movements or changing costs as companies refinance lower rate debt, and may create opportunity as companies must adjust to a higher cost of capital. Investors could potentially benefit from more balanced outcomes along the risk/reward spectrum as we move past the era of excessively low returns for lower risk. The significant strength of the U.S. dollar in the fourth quarter and investors’ concerns about the possible impact of higher tariffs with the incoming Trump administration drove valuations down in the fourth quarter. Given this environment, we see several reasons investors potentially may find opportunities in international equities: Recent performance dispersion increases the potential benefit of diversification to high-quality, global competitors at lower valuations. Volatility in currencies and differentiation of action across central banks create opportunities for international investors. The potential for reversal of U.S. dollar strength could favor increased allocations to international equities. We see significant value in many constituents in the MSCI EAFE Index, particularly among global multi-nationals, as negative expectations for the impact of tariffs seem overdone and the market appears to be affording little value to revenues from U.S. sales following the U.S. dollar’s strong move in the quarter.

data.title: Darling Ingredients: Benefiting From Protectionist Policies And Biofuel Incentives
data.description:
Darling Ingredients (DAR) is one of the most compelling stocks in the circular economy sector. The US company processes animal by-products and other types of food waste, using them to produce biofuels through the Diamond Green Diesel (DGD) JV and as natural ingredients for end-markets including food, healthcare and animal feed.

Although FY24 was a challenging year in terms of both revenue and margins, I believe that the new regulatory environment, characterized by the introduction of the Clean Fuel Production Credit, will act as a tailwind for the company, supporting its growth as early as FY25. In addition, the Trump administration has included biofuels among the key energy sources for the country's development, as opposed to solar and wind, which are absent within the release. A more protectionist approach to expanding the energy sector will favor US companies at the expense of foreign competitors, benefiting the competitive position of Darling Ingredients and DGD.

Management's strategy oriented toward reducing leverage, which has increased as a result of significant acquisitions between FY22 and FY23, is consistent with the high-interest rate environment and although it may slow growth in the short term due to lower CAPEX, it will enable greater operational flexibility in the coming years. Considering the recent regulatory developments and the DCF results indicating a potential upside of more than 30%, I assign Darling Ingredients a "buy" rating.

Business Overview

Darling Ingredients is one of the global leaders in the valorization of food waste and animal by-products focused on the production of fats, oils, proteins, food and feed ingredients and biofuels. The company's business is organized around 3 segments.

Feed

The Feed segment accounted for 64.3% of revenue in FY24 and consists of the production of fats, proteins and used cooking oil, which are used as feed for farm animals and to produce biofuels. In FY24, 26% of the sales came from the DGD JV, which uses these products as a base for the production of renewable diesel and SAF. This dynamic fosters significant vertical integration, allowing Darling to achieve higher margins by leveraging a higher value-added sector than animal feed and DGD to obtain low-cost feedstock, increasing its level of competitiveness.

The uncertain regulatory environment in FY24, coupled with a significant increase in feedstock supply from abroad, negatively affected revenue, down 17.8% from FY23, and caused a significant reduction in margins.

Nevertheless, the new technical details related to the 45Z tax credit, released in January 2025, portend a positive development of the segment starting in FY25. They eliminate the possibility of qualifying for the tax credit on imported RD or on RD produced through imported UCO, limiting its import from China and Brazil, among the main contributors to market saturation in 2024.

The tax credit will be adjusted according to the carbon intensity of the feedstock used and Darling's is one of the lowest in the market, a factor that may not only increase DGD's margins but also encourage sales to other domestic producers of RD and SAF. These considerations imply a significant increase in demand for the Feed segment starting in FY25, as well as a likely price increase driven by lower market feedstock supply. For these reasons, I expect a substantial revenue and operating margin improvement as early as FY25.

Food

The Food segment accounted for 26% of Darling's sales and is involved in food fat recovery and collagen production for the food, pharmaceutical, and other technical industries such as photography. In contrast to the Feed segment, the business unit has more geographic revenue diversification, spread across Europe, North America, and China. In FY24 the segment reported 15% YoY declining sales primarily due to price dynamics, while maintaining only slightly lower margins than in FY23.

The most interesting development concerns the introduction of Nexida GC, a Collagen Peptide that, according to data reported by the company, lowers post-meal glucose spikes in the blood by an average of 42%. The product is receiving a good market response with accelerating demand.

The business unit's target end-markets make it the segment with the lowest revenue volatility, providing a solid base for the company. For this reason, in FY25, I expect revenue and margins to remain stable or increase slightly.

The business unit's target end-markets make it the segment with the lowest volatile revenue, providing a solid base for the company. For this reason, in FY25 I expect revenue and margins to remain stable or slightly increase.

Fuel

The Fuel segment accounted for 9.63% of Darling's sales and consists mainly of the 50-50 JV DGD with Valero, as well as dealing with RNG production in Europe. I believe the business unit will be the area with the greatest growth opportunities and the main contributor to the company's operating margin and profitability improvement from FY25 onwards as a result of the implementation of the Clean Fuel Production Credit.

The new regulations provide a tax credit of $1/gallon for non-aviation fuel production and $1.75/gallon for SAF production. In addition, as previously anticipated, the credit is multiplied based on the CI of the feedstock used, a factor that will allow DGD to qualify for a higher credit than other competitors such as Neste (OTCPK:NTOIF), strengthening its competitive position. The major change related to the new regulation consists in the protectionist structure envisioned by the Trump administration that will make the 45Z tax credit available only to domestic biofuels producers. This development will significantly benefit Darling for 2 reasons:

It will disincentivize the import of RD and SAF, reducing their market supply, which will have positive effects on RINs (D4) and LCFS prices, thereby supporting DGD's profitability.

It will expand demand for feedstock (Feed segment) produced in the US, where Darling is the leading domestic supplier.

Moreover, while the tax credit for RD production will remain aligned with the Biodiesel Tax Credit in effect until December 2024, the new subsidy provides a significant economic benefit for SAF production within US. For this reason, Darling and Valero, through a $315 million investment have converted part of the Port Arthur plant's RD production to SAF, resulting in a production capacity of 235 million gallons per year, which can be further expanded in the future. Although SAF demand is not high enough to justify full utilization of the plant for the moment, it would mean 45z tax credits of more than $400 million upon full capacity utilization.

Financial Snapshot

In FY24 Darling reported a 15.8% YoY revenue decline, driven by a general reduction in selling prices, which also resulted in lower operating margins. EBIT margin of 8.19% was significantly lower than the 13.99% achieved in FY23. A sustained increase in depreciation and a drop in dividends received by DGD, down to $149 million in FY24 from $366 million in the previous year, contributed to this decline. Despite this, in January 2025 alone, Darling received a dividend of $86 million from DGD, suggesting significantly better dynamics for FY25.

The acquisitions of Gelnex, FASA Group, and Valley Proteins between FY22 and FY23 resulted in a significant debt increase, with net debt reaching a high of $4.5 billion as of December 2023. This development led management to prioritize debt reduction through decreased investment. In FY24, the company made $333 million in CAPEX and spent $116 million on acquisitions. These amounts, compared with about $900 million in OCF, allowed Darling to pay down $353 million in debt.

For FY25, management has planned CAPEX of around $400 million, forecasting a debt reduction between $350 million and $500 million, approaching the target Net Debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.5x. Once this debt level is reached, a renewed increase in investment and the possibility to pay adequate remuneration to shareholders, primarily through share buy-back, is likely.

Peers Comparison

A comparison with some peers shows the high competitiveness of Darling, which appears to have higher operating margins and profitability than other reported companies, except for Ingredion Incorporated (INGR) in FY24. This result is achieved due to the company's high vertical integration, which is unique in the industry. In particular, I consider the comparison with Neste relevant, as the company is globally active in RD and SAF production. High market saturation had a very negative impact on the Finnish company, which experienced a gradual reduction in margins between FY21 and FY24, resulting in a loss in the last financial year. New regulations regarding RD and SAF production could also have a strongly negative effect on Neste's FY25 results, all to the benefit of DGD and consequently Darling, allowing it to absorb some of Neste's US demand.

The Price-to-Sales and P/E comparison shows a premium valuation associated by the market to Darling compared to its competitors. I believe this depends mainly on 2 factors:

Darling's high profitability places it in a strong position compared to competitors, allowing the company to generate a profit even in the event of a significant reduction in selling prices.

Darling is characterized by higher expected profit growth than its competitors. The company presents an FY25 P/E (e) of 17.5x, expected to decline to 12.46x for FY26 (e). While the former value is significantly above the competitors' average (12.51x), the FY26 value appears only slightly above the average (11.62x).

Discounted Cash Flow

To attribute a valuation to DAR, I performed a three-stage DCF. I made an estimate of the expected values of revenue, EBIT margin, CAPEX and depreciation for FY25, FY26, FY27. I made a projection of these values for the period between FY28 and FY34, finally calculating the terminal value. This analysis returned a target price of $52.24 per share, which is about 30% higher than DAR current market price. To determine this value, I made the following assumptions:

Beta was obtained using the unlevered beta of "Chemical (Basic)" industry reported by Professor Aswath Damodaran, subsequently calculating levered beta based on DAR D/E ratio. A value of 1.18 was derived.

Equity risk premium of 4.82% was obtained from the weighted average of the ERPs of the countries where DAR operates. Countries' ERPs are provided by Aswath Damodaran. Cost of equity of 10.47% derived from CAPM model.

Risk-free rate of 4.78%, based on average of the 10-year government bonds of the countries where DAR operates.

Cost of debt of 6.00%.

WACC of 8.15%.

The growth rate (g) of 2%.

Main Risks

Many products sold by the company are classified as commodities and therefore are highly dependent on market dynamics that increase their price volatility.

DGD financials, and consequently those of Darling, are highly dependent on tax credits under the Clean Fuel Production Credit and the prices of RINs and LCFS. Changes in current regulations could have a major impact on the company's results.

A commercial war between the US, Europe and China could have a negative impact on the Food segment, which in FY24 earned about $1 billion outside the US.

High debt, if not managed, may lead to reduced margins and limit new borrowings in the future. Despite this, management's strategy focused on debt reduction appears to be able to limit this risk considerably.

Takeaway

I believe that Darling Ingredients represents a very attractive investment considering the regulatory changes concerning biofuels production that are expected to have a very positive impact on the company as early as FY25. An increase in demand and price of feedstock in the Feed segment, coupled with an expected high monetization of the 45Z tax credit by DGD, also as a result of SAF sales represents, in my opinion, the main driver of revenue and profitability growth. In addition, the company has a premium valuation compared to its competitors, evidence of the highly competitive position attributed by the market to the US company. For these reasons, also given the DCF indicating a possible upside of more than 30%, I assign Darling Ingredients a "buy" rating.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Celsius Surges On Alani Nu Deal
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Beverage jolt: Celsius (CELH) skyrockets after inking deal to acquire Alani Nu. (00:24) Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) to seek investment from Tesla (TSLA) after fallout with Honda (HMC) - report. (01:34) YouTube (GOOGL) (GOOG) plans to roll out 'premium lite' in major markets - report. (02:46)

This is an abridged transcript.

Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH) skyrocketed in late trading on Thursday after the company announced an acquisition of Alani Nutrition LLC for $1.8 billion.

Alani Nu was founded in 2018 and is a female-focused brand that offers functional beverages and wellness products targeting Gen Z and millennial consumers.

John Fieldly, Chairman and CEO of Celsius, expressed enthusiasm for the acquisition, highlighting the respect for Alani Nu's strong community of supporters and authentic brand partnerships.

Financially, the acquisition is expected to enhance Celsius' (NASDAQ:CELH) topline growth and also be cash EPS accretive in the first year of ownership. Notably, the company anticipates achieving $50 million in run-rate cost synergies over two years post-close.

The energy drink deal has implications for PepsiCo (PEP), as a holder of a large stake in Celsius (CELH), as well as rivals Coca-Cola (KO), Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), Zevia (ZVIA), and Monster Beverage (MNST), all competing for beverage market share.

Celsius (CELH) is up 35% in premarket action.

A high-level Japanese group, including former Prime Minister Yoshihide Suga, is proposing that Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) invest in Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) following the fallout of merger talks with Honda (HMC).

According to a Financial Times report that cites people with direct knowledge of the matter, this initiative, led by ex-Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) board member Hiro Mizuno, aims to position Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) as a strategic investor, particularly interested in acquiring Nissan's (OTCPK:NSANY) manufacturing plants in the U.S.

Merger talks with Honda (HMC) were terminated last week, which has likely intensified Nissan's (OTCPK:NSANY) search for strategic alliances to bolster operations in the face of ongoing financial challenges.

Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) stock jumped ~10% on the Japanese stock exchange after the news.

Separately, Moody's has downgraded Nissan's (OTCPK:NSANY) rating to junk, saying the decision "reflects Nissan's weak profitability driven by slowing demand for its aging model portfolio".

"Even if the company successfully executes its restructuring plan with cost reductions and new model releases, we do not expect free cash flow to turn positive until fiscal 2026 at the earliest," Moody's added.

YouTube (GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is planning to introduce a new, lower-priced version of its paid video service.

Bloomberg reported Thursday that the service will target viewers who primarily want to watch programs other than music videos.

The “premium lite” YouTube plan will be announced soon in the U.S., Australia, Germany, and Thailand, the report said, citing a person familiar with the plans.

The Verge previously reported that the premium lite plan was being tested in overseas markets for months. The plan is now expected to officially roll out more broadly, including in the U.S. for the first time.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

New No. 1: Amazon takes the quarterly revenue crown from Walmart

Walgreens Boots drops amid report Sycamore bidding for Family Dollar chain

Lilly's next obesity drug serves up dramatic weight loss -- and side effects: report

Catalyst watch:

  • Sandisk (SDK) will start trading after being spun off from Western Digital (WDC). The business split will create two independent, publicly traded companies focused on different aspects of data storage technology.

  • The CAGNY Conference will include presentations from Celsius Holdings (CELH) and Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGY).

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is down 0.8% at $71/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1.2% at $98,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.1% and the DAX is flat.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Block (NYSE:XYZ) shares tumbled nearly 7% after missing Q4 earnings estimates and issuing a disappointing outlook for Q1 and FY2025.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Existing Home Sales

  • 10:00 am Quarterly Services Survey

  • 11:30 am San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will give opening remarks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

  • 11:30 am Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson will speak on "Central Bank Communication" before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.
