data.title: Garmin Q4 2024: Record Revenue Masks Underlying Risks
data.description:
After releasing headline-grabbing Q4 2024 Earnings results, I think it's a great time to check back into how things are going with the wearable technology company, Garmin Ltd. (NYSE:GRMN). The last time I took a bullish stance on the company was back in November 2023, because of its cost and time-efficient vertically integrated business model and its investment into new product releases and diversification. Since then, the stock price has increased by 108.56% and the top and bottom-line results have shown upward trending results for three consecutive financial years.

In Q4 2024 we saw record revenue, an EPS increase of 40% and the company generously increasing its dividend by 20%. The recent headlines and historic performance look good. If the stock were cheaper, I would stick to my bullish recommendation. But at its current premium valuation, the potential impact of competition, its loss-making Auto OEM segment and the rising geopolitical risks in Taiwan are not worth the risk at this price point. Therefore, as a cautious investor, I would recommend a wait-and-see hold rating.

Q4 2024 Recap

Garmin has without a doubt released some impressive results to finish off its financial year with a 20% YoY increase in sales to hit $1.82 billion. This is a company that is not just relying on its existing product line, it is investing in product innovation across a diverse range of segments, frequently releasing new products in the last year. Just a quick Google and your search results will be filled with product releases across a wide range of segments. This diversification has been a key driver of revenue growth. We can see double-digit growth across a number of sectors, including Fitness in which revenue grew by 31% YoY, Outdoor which grew by 29% thanks to a high demand for adventure watches and Auto OEM which saw revenue growth of 30% YoY.

Unfortunately, Auto OEM becomes an eyesore when looking at operating income. Despite its revenue growth, the segment had an operating loss of $39 million due to a 10% increase in operating expenses and higher-than-expected foreign currency losses. Conversely, the loss is only a small percentage of the total income and as a result, there is little to fault in its FY 2024 performance.

However, due to the high valuation, I wanted to take a more critical look into the potential cracks.

Potential company concerns

If we look further than just the financial results, three potential issues stand out when analysing Garmin's future performance. The first concern is namely competition within its Fitness segment from big-name competitors in the form of Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). While Garmin's Fitness segment income growth grew by 108% the highly competitive market is showing signs of saturation as seen below in the graph. The fight for market share could put pressure on pricing and negatively impact margins.

The second concern lies within its Auto AEM segment, which has shown persistent losses YoY in FY2024. Management indicated softened demand from major automakers. A few years, back, it was believed that this segment would bring in $800 million in 2025. This will be far from the reality with a guidance of 7% revenue growth for FY2025, and the question arises whether a potential divestiture or maybe even a partnership could improve the value here.

Lastly, the company has seen soft demand within its Marine segment. We could see a 17% YoY revenue growth, but only 6% was due to organic growth, most of the growth came from the JL Audio acquisition. Although it is great to see the boost that this acquisition has given to total revenue, investors should still be wary about the potential integration costs.

Valuation

Garmin is a company that has proven itself a sturdy long-term stock to hold, with returns of 362.82% over the last decade. Like many companies on the stock market, it took a Covid-19 hit, but we can now see that the stock is trading at historical highs. If we look at the valuation, we can see that the stock valuation is much higher than its five-year average across all metrics, not including the FWD PEG non-GAAP of 1.86. A lot of this valuation is based on the strong momentum and growth the company experienced in FY2024. However, from the company's guidance and concerns previously addressed, it appears unlikely that the company can repeat the same set of results in FY2025.

Garmin's guidance shows an 8% revenue growth and an EPS of $7.80 for FY2025, which aligns with analyst consensus. However, there are definitely some risks to consider for the upcoming year.

Risks

A risk that investors may want to be wary of is that a very large part of the company's manufacturing takes place in Taiwan. As it stands right now, we are still not sure to what extent the rising tensions between the US and China tariff relations could potentially disrupt and seriously hurt the company's financial performance. Another issue is that there appear to be growing issues with software updates and neglect of its legacy products. Software glitches and issues with the technology are well documented on the internet and could negatively impact its financial performance if these are neglected or issues continue. Lastly, the company increased its dividend by 20%, while pleasing investors, it could hurt the company in the long run if they neglect to invest this money into R&D for innovations, necessary to stay relevant in a competitive market.

Final thoughts

On a financial front, the company has delivered a strong performance, and it is great to see three financial years of top and bottom-line growth. On top of that, the company has rewarded investors with a dividend hike of 20%. However, the stock price has increased incredibly over the last year and the premium pricing warrants more caution. With a more critical eye, tough competition, loss-making segments and softening demand in FY2025 make the valuation harder to chew. Therefore, for now, I recommend a hold rating until we have better clarity as to a potential Auto OEM turnaround and the demand at play in 2025.
